APPLICATION DETAILS.Raw Content:
Research title
UNderstanding and Improving communication about blood TEsts in primary care (UNITE): a
multi-method study using ethnographic and participatory approaches
Research career to date (1000/1000 words)
Research you have led or made a significant contribution to (making clear what your role was), the
research methods you have experience of, and the impact and outputs of the research you have been
involved in (linking to details given in your CV). Any other relevant experience such as supervisory,
management, teaching, public engagement and involvement, or leadership experience. Other skills
and experience which highlights your suitability for the fellowship and demonstrates your potential as
a future health or social care research leader
Academic Clinical Fellowship, KCL (2021-2025)
I have been a Research Fellow since August 2023 on UKRI-funded Social Responses to Stigma
project, led by Dr Andrew Guise. This ethnographic study examines how stigma and discrimination
arise within the health and care services used by people who are homeless in south London. My work
includes ethnographic observation in day centres and collaborative analysis of fieldnotes and
interviews.
Outputs:
- I lead a research strand exploring the intersection of stigma, homelessness and health self-
management. I presented this work at the RCGP Conference 2024 and am preparing a first-
author paper for submission to Sociology of Health and Illness.
- I co-authored a paper on a nested quantitative survey of homeless people’s stigma
experiences, under review at BMJ Open.
- I am the co-author of a forthcoming submission on drug use stigma (International Journal of
Drug Policy).
In 2022, I collaborated with early-career researchers at Charité University Berlin and the Medical
University of Vienna on a European University Hospitals Alliance project to improve Type 2 diabetes
prevention in women with gestational diabetes. We secured a £10,000 seed grant and convened
workshops in London, Berlin and Vienna. I am the first author of our interdisciplinary commentary
paper, currently under review at Journal of Midwifery and Women’s Health.
Other activities:
- I am studying for a PGCert in Applied Research Methods at KCL, due for completion June
2025. I have been awarded Distinction in all completed modules.
- I am an advisory board member (2022-) for PREPARED, a multi-method study of a primary
care intervention to improve pregnancy outcomes in women with diabetes.
- I was invited by Professor David Misselbrook to be a peer editor for his textbook, Thinking
About Medicine: An Introduction to the Philosophy of Medicine, providing a critical review of
each chapter.
- I am a peer reviewer for publications including Social Science and Medicine, British Journal of
General Practice, Journal of Medical Ethics, BMC Medical Ethics, Digital Health, and
Information, Communication & Society.
MPhil, University of Cambridge (2019-2020)
My interdisciplinary MPhil (First Class with Distinction) combined medical philosophy, sociology and
anthropology. I received a Starred Distinction for my thesis, a qualitative interview study exploring how
participating in an online gestational diabetes support community shaped women’s experiences of

pregnancy. I published this work as a single-author publication in Sociology of Health and Illness
(2022) and presented it at three international conferences, including FIGO World Congress (2021).
Overdiagnosis research (2019-)
During the MPhil I became interested in how clinicians negotiate risk and uncertainty and
communicate these issues to patients. I published my research on how to explain the pitfalls of
screening to patients in Journal of Medical Ethics as a single-author paper, which scored in the top 5%
of research outputs on Altmetric. I was invited to present this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in the Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians
balance risk in end-of-life care decisions. I was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for best Diploma
performance and invited to give a public lecture on my dissertation research. I published this work as
single-author in Journal of Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022) and presented at Preventing
Overdiagnosis 2023. I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and
am organising a pre-conference workshop for the 2025 Oxford conference.
Previous research experience
As an Honorary Research Fellow at QMUL (2017-2019), I led a systematic review of post-partum
depression and intimate partner violence in high-income countries, winning 1st prize at the British
Society for Biopsychosocial Obstetrics and Gynaecology conference (2018). I co-authored a
systematic review and meta-analysis of health service use by people with severe mental illness,
published in PLOS Medicine (2021). I also led the quantitative analysis of the LGBT Foundation’s
survey of women who have sex with women (2019), the results of which were presented at the All-
Party Parliamentary Group on Sexual and Reproductive Health.
For my MSc dissertation I conducted a quantitative analysis of the National Survey of Sexual Attitudes
and Lifestyles, focusing on birth order and sexual health outcomes. This work – published as a first-
author paper in Sex Education (2018) – attracted significant media attention, including coverage in the
Independent and the Daily Mail.
(https://www.independent.co.uk/news/education/education-news/sex-education-first-born-children-
parents-families-london-school-hygiene-topical-medicine-a8558211.html).
At medical school I co-led a national survey of medical students’ views on abortion teaching, which I
presented at two international conferences (2016). I was subsequently invited to the Royal College of
Obstetricians and Gynaecologists to discuss curriculum recommendations. I also led a hospital-based
audit of the risk assessment of venous thrombo-embolism (VTE) in pregnancy which led to the
hospital updating its VTE guidelines; I presented this work at two national conferences (2015).
Teaching and management
In 2018, I co-designed and delivered a critical appraisal module (11 weeks) for Global Health BSc
students at QMUL. I was awarded the Foundation School Award for Teaching Excellence in
recognition of my mentorship of medical students applying for Academic Foundation posts. I have held
Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy since 2019.
As chair of the Cambridge Global Health Lecture committee (2019-2020), I coordinated a series of six
public lectures, attracting over 200 attendees. From 2015-2016, I was Policy and Advocacy Director
for Students for Global Health UK, leading a team of policy analysts and steering four national student
research collaborations.
Impact of fellowship
An NIHR Doctoral Research Fellowship will allow me to continue my academic career and develop as
a future research leader. My eventual goal is to become a Professor of Primary Care, using
interdisciplinary qualitative methods to explore what good primary care looks like in practice. The

proposed project will develop my skills in complex qualitative methodologies and allow me to build my
portfolio of research addressing medical overuse. Following the PhD, I hope to secure post-doctoral
funding (e.g. an Academic Clinical Lectureship) to conduct further ethnographic work exploring the
social context of medical overuse. I am especially interested in co-producing creative outputs which
can engage wider audiences to consider the wise use of medical resources.
Plain English summary (628/600 words)
Aims, background, design and methods used, PPI involvement, dissemination.
Background
The number of blood tests ordered in general practices has tripled since 2000. Blood tests can be
useful but do not always make patient care better. They can sometimes cause problems: for example,
borderline blood tests may mean that patients worry or have unnecessary further tests. General
practice staff don’t always talk clearly about these issues, so patients may have different expectations
of blood tests.
Research suggests that communication about blood tests in general practice could be improved.
Patients say they sometimes feel uncertain about why tests are done and what results mean. General
practitioners (GPs) often make decisions to do blood tests without much input from patients, who feel
they don’t get enough chance to discuss blood tests and results.
Improving communication between general practice staff and patients about blood tests could reduce
unnecessary testing and help patients make more informed decisions.
Aims
This project will help general practice staff and patients talk more clearly about blood tests, so patients
are more involved in deciding whether to have tests and can understand their results better.
Research design
This project has two parts:
1. Understanding how blood tests are discussed :
i. Evidence search: I will look at existing research on how blood tests are discussed in
general practices to understand what we know so far and what is missing.
ii. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations
about blood tests at two different general practices. This will include patient
consultations. I will make recordings with the permission of staff and patients.
2. Improving how blood tests are discussed :
i. Workshops with general practice staff: I show short clips of the recordings to practice
staff. They will discuss their worries about how blood tests are discussed and think
about how to improve communication.
ii. Forum Theatre workshops with the public: I will work with a theatre group and public
contributors to create a short play based on the findings of the project. The play will be
performed to members of the public as part of a “Forum Theatre” workshop, in which
attendees are invited to make changes to the play to improve how blood tests are
discussed. With the support of public contributors, the play will be developed into two
tools:
 a workshop to help general practice staff speak more clearly about
blood tests
 an online resource for patients to help patients feel more confident
discussing blood tests and understanding their results.
Patient and public involvement (PPI)
People who have recently had blood tests helped design this research and will stay involved
throughout. This means the research can respond to the needs and preferences of people having GP

blood tests. To develop this proposal, I held three workshops with eighteen members of the public.
They described many issues with communication about blood tests and felt strongly that this research
was important.
Members of the public will be part of a PPI panel, meeting three times a year to guide the project by:
 reviewing consent forms/study information leaflets;
 helping to analyse data;
 writing summaries of the research for the public.
They will be paid for their contributions and will receive funding for training. They will also have the
chance to help share the research through conference presentations. Two lay people will co-chair a
separate Project Advisory Group to oversee the project.
Sharing the research
The research will be shared with local community groups, GP practice staff and patients via social
media, blogs, a podcast, and a public event. The workshop tool created from the play will be used to
train GP staff and trainees on better blood test communication. I will work with the PPI panel to
develop leaflets and social media resources about the research, which will be made available online
along with the play.

Scientific abstract (500/500 words)
Research question, background, aims & objectives, methods, timelines for delivery, anticipated
impact and dissemination.
Background
Primary care clinicians order nearly half of all NHS blood tests, with primary care testing rates tripling
between 2000-2016. Blood tests place significant demand on GP time and budgets but do not always
improve patient care. Research suggests that communication about blood tests in primary care is
often inadequate. The limitations of blood tests are seldom explained to patients, who report that they
are not always involved in blood testing decisions and often do not understand what their results
mean. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these communication issues need
urgent attention. This research will use ethnographic methods to explore the role of communication in
shaping blood testing practices and improve how blood tests are discussed in primary care settings.
Aim
To understand how GPs and other primary care clinicians discuss blood tests with patients and how
this communication might be improved.
Research questions
a. How is the issue of blood testing communication framed in the existing literature?
b. What do clinicians and patients seek to accomplish with blood tests and how is this
communicated?
a. How are decisions about ordering blood tests negotiated and communicated in
practice?
b. How do clinicians and patients communicate what they understand about blood test
results?
c. What concerns do primary care clinicians have about blood testing communication, and how
can insights from participatory ethnography support clinicians to identify solutions to these
concerns?
d. How can participatory creative methods be harnessed to support more effective
communication between patients and primary care clinicians about blood tests?
Methods
Phase 1: Understanding blood test communication
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on blood testing
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two GP practices with contrasting patient
demographics, observing the entire blood testing process with a focus on patient consultations
where blood tests are discussed.
Phase 2: Improving blood test communication
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with GP staff to identify concerns and challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Video-recorded Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and GPs. The
recorded performances will be adapted into a workshop resource (for clinicians) and an online
resource (for patients/the public) to support improved blood test communication in primary care.
Timelines
 Year 1: meta-ethnography, ethics application
 Year 2: detailed ethnographic observation and video-reflexive ethnography workshops
 Year 3: creative co-production of patient and clinician resources
Anticipated impact
This participatory research project will reframe the GP consultation as a key site of opportunity for the
prudent use of blood testing. It will engage GP practice staff, lay people and theatre-makers to co-
produce resources to support patients and primary care clinicians to communicate better about blood
tests.

Dissemination
The workshop resource developed in WP4 will be shared with local primary care staff and GP trainees,
as well as at national conferences. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-
accessible resource for patients. Research findings will be published in academic journals and shared
via social media, blogs and podcasts.

Detailed research plan (5250/5000 words)
1. What is the problem?
Nearly half of all NHS blood tests are carried out in primary care, and primary care test requests tripled
between 2000-2016.1 Primary care blood testing is expensive1 and managing test results contributes
significantly to GP workload.2 Despite their financial and administrative burden, blood tests do not
always improve patient care: many blood tests do not change clinical management, and up to 30% of
blood tests may be unnecessary.3 Blood tests, like any medical intervention, can also cause harm.
False-positive or borderline results may cause unnecessary anxiety or lead to further unwarranted
procedures, while normal results may provide false reassurance which can delay diagnosis.
It is important that primary care staff involve patients in decisions about blood testing and explain
clearly what test results mean. Yet many patients say they struggle to understand their results and do
not get a choice about whether or not to do blood tests.4 While GPs acknowledge that tests are not
always useful and can sometimes lead to more uncertainty,5 they often initiate blood tests without
discussing alternatives or explaining the potential risks and limitations.6 This limits opportunities for
patients to be involved in their healthcare and make the right decisions for them.
NHS England has produced guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care, but these focus on
clinical appropriateness of blood tests; 7 the guidelines do not address how communication about
blood testing should proceed, nor how to explain the risks and pitfalls of testing to patients.
Recommendations about which tests to perform are useful, but cannot replace the need for GP staff to
elicit patients’ expectations of blood tests and explain the reasoning behind testing decisions.
Examining what happens in GP consultations about blood tests is crucial to understanding the rising
demand for blood testing in primary care. However, research which directly explores these
interactions is limited, meaning that there is a gap in our understanding of how communication about
blood testing might be improved. This research will use ethnographic methods to explore how
conversations about blood tests influence testing practices in primary care. By observing real-world
interactions between clinicians and patients in situ, this project aims to identify ways to improve blood
test communication and support more prudent, patient-centred testing practices.
2. Why is this research important?
This research is the first to use ethnographic methods to explore how blood tests are discussed in GP
settings. The project will open the ‘black box’ of blood test discussions by directly observing
communication between GP staff and patients, providing important insight into how communication
can sustain or challenge inappropriate blood test use in the GP setting. Through the use of innovative,
participatory methods, GP staff and patients will be invited to identify practical solutions to improve
blood test communication.
This research is timely. The NHS Long Term Plan stipulates that all patients must have access to their
online records – including test results – by the end of 2024,8 a development which has significant
implications for blood test communication. Previous research shows that that online access can result
in unintended consequences, including patient anxiety about blood test results and increased
workload.9,10 This project will explore these issues in-depth, providing critical insight into how digital
access has shaped blood test communication.
Ensuring that blood tests are used wisely is important for the sustainability of primary care. Primary
care clinicians request 45% of all NHS blood tests, at a cost of approximately £1.8bn in 2015-16.1
Unnecessary blood tests can lead to costly follow-up care such as outpatient visits and hospital
investigations.11 By encouraging GP staff to speak more clearly about the limitations of blood tests,
this research can help to reduce overuse and lead to much-needed cost-savings for the NHS.
The project will also seek to alleviate the pressures faced by GP staff. A survey by the Royal College
of General Practitioners (RCGP) found that three-quarters of GPs feel patient safety is being
compromised by excessive workloads,12 and the British Medical Association has warned of ‘a growing
crisis’ in primary care.13 By identifying strategies to improve blood testing practices, this research will

free up GP consultation time and reduce the administrative burden associated with managing blood
test results.
This work aligns with NIHR’s ‘Prevention and Public Health’ priority research theme for 2024-25 by
seeking to understand how blood test investigations can be used in primary care to provide maximum
benefit with the least harm. The project will build on a number of NIHR-funded projects on primary care
blood tests by providing rich ethnographic insights into how communication shapes blood testing
practices.
3. Review of existing evidence
The volume of blood testing in primary care is a financial and logistical burden, and many of the blood
tests ordered in primary care appear to be of little diagnostic or prognostic value. In the Why Test
study, which reviewed blood test results for 2572 patients across 57 UK GP practices, only 6.2% of
tests led to a new or confirmed diagnosis, while nearly half of tests (48.8%) did not result in patient
reassurance or any change in management. Furthermore, a quarter of tests were felt to be partially or
fully unnecessary when retrospectively reviewed by another clinician.3
Increased blood testing exposes patients to the risk of overdiagnosis, which causes harm through
further investigations, treatments, or anxiety. The harms of blood tests may be overlooked in
comparison to more invasive diagnostic procedures, but blood tests are not benign interventions.
Borderline results are common – in one study of GP blood tests, only 26.6% of patients had completely
normal blood test results3 – and can lead to unnecessary anxiety, particularly if patients access these
results via online apps.10 False-positive or borderline results can also lead to ‘investigation
momentum’: further testing or invasive procedures such as biopsies and irradiating scans.14
Alternatively, normal blood test results in a patient presenting with symptoms may provide undue
reassurance which delays diagnosis, or leads focus away from discussing lifestyle changes.15
Despite the limitations of testing, patients tend to have high expectations for blood tests as a
diagnostic tool18,19 and may underestimate the potential harms.20 Patients may request blood tests as
part of a general ‘check-up’18 despite limited evidence for the value of testing in healthy asymptomatic
individuals.21,22 A UK-based qualitative study by Watson et al. interviewed GPs and patients at the time
of blood testing and again after test results were received, finding that there was often a mismatch in
patients’ and GP’s expectations of testing.4 Patients felt that blood tests would provide answers to and
solutions for their symptoms, and perceived the offer of testing as a sign that the doctor was taking
their concerns seriously. By contrast, GPs tended to be less optimistic about blood tests, viewing them
as a way to rule out serious problems rather than pin down specific diagnoses. Even if they feel blood
tests are unlikely to be of clinical use, clinicians may acquiesce to patient requests in order to avoid
lengthy discussions about the merits of testing.15
Communication between clinicians and patients appears to be an important driver of medical overuse.
Research into antibiotic prescribing practices in primary care, for example, suggests that clinicians
often mistake expressions of concern about symptoms as requests for antibiotic treatment.16 Similar
patterns may underlie overuse of blood tests, as in research which suggests that clinicians may initiate
testing to satisfy or affirm patients even where there has been no explicit patient request.17
Although there is a wide body of research using directly-observed data to examine communication in
primary care consultations, 25–30 there is only one UK-based study of this kind which focuses on blood
test discussions: a secondary content analysis of 27 audio-recordings from an archive of GP
consultations, which showed that shared decision-making was rarely evident in blood test
discussions.6 While the study quantified various aspects of poor communication – for example, that
patients were given the opportunity to ask questions about blood test results only 13% of the time –
there remains a gap in the literature for a more detailed and in-depth account of blood test
communication using naturalistic data. This will help us to better understand the interactional factors
that contribute to increased testing rates, as well as the difficulties patients report in understanding
their blood test results.

4. Aims/research questions
Aim
To understand how GPs and other primary care clinicians discuss blood tests with patients, and how
this communication might be improved to support appropriate use of blood testing in primary care.
Research questions
1. How is the issue of blood testing communication framed in the existing literature?
2. What do clinicians and patients seek to accomplish with blood tests and how is this
communicated?
a. How are decisions about ordering blood tests negotiated and communicated in
practice?
b. How do clinicians and patients communicate what they understand about blood test
results?
3. What concerns do primary care clinicians have about blood testing communication, and how
can insights from participatory ethnography support clinicians to identify solutions to these
concerns?
4. How can participatory creative methods be harnessed to support more effective
communication between patients and primary care clinicians about blood tests?
5. Project plan
Phase 1: understanding blood test communication in primary care
Work Package (WP) 1: Synthesising existing qualitative literature about primary care blood
testing discussions.
Objectives
1. Identify when, where and how blood tests are discussed in primary care.
2. Explore how patient and clinicians’ perspectives on and experiences of blood testing are
framed in the existing literature.
3. Develop new conceptual understandings of blood test communication in primary care settings.
Methods
I will conduct a meta-ethnography of qualitative literature exploring communication about blood tests in
a primary care setting. Meta-ethnography is a form of interpretative qualitative synthesis, involving
detailed re-interpretation of the primary data and themes in published literature.31 Unlike other
aggregative qualitative synthesis approaches, meta-ethnography aims to develop new theories about
the topic under study.32 Meta-ethnography is particularly useful for synthesising small numbers of
papers, and I have identified <20 qualitative studies on this topic.
I will follow the seven stages of meta-ethnography described by Noblit and Hare: (1) getting started;
(2) deciding what is relevant; (3) reading the studies; (4) determining how studies are related; (5)
translating the findings; (6) synthesising translations; (7) expressing the synthesis.33
Search strategy
I will conduct a systematic literature search of medical, social science and public health databases,
using appropriate search terms and operator functions as guided by a QMUL research librarian. I will
also search grey literature via thesis repositories, Google Scholar, citation searches, and reference list
reviews. In order to keep the review relevant to contemporary practice, I will restrict papers to those
published (in English) within the past 15 years.

Studies will be imported into Mendeley Reference Manager. After removing duplicates I will screen the
titles and abstracts of the remaining papers against SPIDER criteria:34
 Sample: staff members working in primary care; patients accessing these services.
 Phenomenon of Interest: blood testing
 Design: interview, focus groups, case studies, ethnography.
 Evaluation: motivations, thoughts, beliefs, understandings.
 Research type: qualitative (including mixed-methods).
I will screen the full texts of selected papers from the first round of screening. A random sample of 50%
of these papers will be double-screened by a second reviewer (e.g. Professor Swinglehurst). We will
meet to discuss and resolve any disagreements.
Data extraction
Study characteristics will be extracted and imported into a shared spreadsheet. The second reviewer
and I will undertake quality appraisal of each study using Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) criteria for
qualitative research.35
Through repeated readings, the second reviewer and I will extract data from each study, capturing
themes, first-order constructs (participant quotes) and second-order constructs (the study authors’
interpretations).
Data analysis and synthesis
I will work with a second reviewer throughout the complex analytical stages (4-6) of the meta-
ethnography. We will hold analysis sessions at each stage with qualitative researchers within the
QMUL APOLLO team to ensure rigour.
In stage 4, relationships between studies will be identified by iteratively grouping related concepts and
themes. In stage 5, findings will be translated by mapping concepts from one study to another, using a
constant comparative method to identify where findings converge or diverge. Finally, in stage 6,
shared themes and concepts from across studies will be integrated into third-order constructs to
develop a ‘line of argument’.31
When writing up the findings of the meta-ethnography I will use the eMERGe reporting framework
developed by France et al.36
WP2: Understanding blood testing practices and interactions in the primary care setting.
To use ethnographic methods to explore in-depth:
1. How decisions to do blood tests are negotiated between patients and GP clinical staff.
2. The role of language and non-verbal communication in interactions about blood tests.
3. GPs’ decision-making processes regarding blood test results.
4. Patients’ experiences of using digital tools to access blood test results.
5. How and whether uncertainty around blood tests is communicated.
6. The approaches used by GP staff to convey and discuss blood test results.
Methods
I will conduct an ethnographic study of blood testing practices and communication in GP practices.
Ethnography is a qualitative method involving immersion in a particular setting through observation,
interviews, reflexive diaries and fieldnotes. It is increasingly recognised as a useful method for primary
care research.2,37–40
Ethnography provides valuable insights into the everyday practices of clinicians and patients,
revealing practices, beliefs and behaviours which may be taken for granted or viewed as
unremarkable.41,42 It can uncover the complexity in routine interactions such as blood test discussions
by capturing meaningful details that people might not remember or mention in interview studies.43
Ethnographic approaches have been used in previous research to develop new understandings of
how medical overuse arises and is sustained through social interactions, norms and practices.41,44,45

Sampling
Observation will take place at two different GP practices to facilitate comparison whilst still allowing for
a long period of in-depth observation in each site. Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to
explore blood testing practices in non-urban settings and in diverse patient groups. I will therefore
recruit an inner-city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population and a suburban practice with an
older patient population. I will use General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites.
46
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months, conducting
approximately 200 hours of observation. Modes of data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
 This will include clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional
meetings, and Patient Participant Groups. To capture the full range of testing practices
I will observe consultations with phlebotomists and nurses as well as GPs. I will make
detailed fieldnotes during and after observations.
2. Opportunistic ethnographic interviews with patients and GP staff
 These informal interviews may be recorded (with consent) using a video recorder, or
will form part of my fieldnotes.
 In order to capture digital aspects of communication about blood tests, I will conduct
ad-hoc think-aloud interviews with patients and staff, asking interviewees to describe
their thoughts and impressions as they use digital resources.2,47
3. Gathering documents from electronic records which relate to blood testing (e.g. consultation
notes detailing discussions about blood tests, screen captures of patient apps).
4. Recording consultations
 I will record approximately 45 consultations/think-aloud interviews in which blood tests
are discussed. To ensure diversity of the dataset I will ensure to include consultations
where interpreters are used. I will aim for a spread of recordings across the testing
process:
i. discussing/ordering tests;
ii. receiving/filing blood test results;
iii. communicating results to patients.
Consent
I will seek consent from clinicians and patients to record consultations either prospectively or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems).
Participants in PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded
consultations and felt patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will
consider this in the consent procedure, guided by the PPI panel and in collaboration with research
sites.
Phase 2: Improving blood test communication in primary care
WP3: Exploring and addressing professional concerns around blood test communication.
1. Elicit the perspectives of primary care staff on communication about blood tests.
2. Support primary care staff to develop new ways of managing blood test communication.
Methods
Video-reflexive ethnography (VRE) is an innovative participatory research method. It involves filming
people in situ doing their usual work, and then showing short clips to relevant actors in facilitated

workshops.48 Participants in VRE workshops watch these recordings to see familiar practices from a
fresh perspective, stimulating discussions about what they may have taken for granted and where
they might see possibilities for change. VRE therefore directly engages those delivering care in the
process of data analysis and quality improvement.
VRE has been used to improve practice in various complex healthcare settings, including maternity
services, 49, acute medical units,50 and dementia care.51 My supervisors Professor Swinglehurst and Dr
Fudge have successfully used this methodology to explore structured medication reviews in GP
practices.52
VRE workshops will be conducted according to the four key principles laid out by Iedema et al.: 48
1. Exnovation: acknowledging the complexity of existing practices and encouraging innovation
with existing resources.
2. Collaboration: valuing the experiences and expertise of participants and supporting a process
of co-creation.
3. Reflexivity: encouraging participants to re-evaluate their assumptions, beliefs and practices.
4. Care: ensuring that participants and researchers feel safe and supported.
Data collection
I will hold six one-hour workshops (three per site), each involving 6-10 primary care staff members. I
will work with practice managers/GP partners to schedule these workshops in time set aside for
training in order to minimise disruption to clinical activities.
In each workshop I will show selected short recordings from WP2 to prompt critical discussions about
blood testing conversations in primary care. These discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed
for analysis. VRE workshops and analysis will take place concurrently, allowing findings from one
workshop to inform the selection of recordings for the next.
The focus of the workshops will be on blood test communication as a ‘critical moment’ for ensuring
appropriate use of primary care resources and facilitating patient involvement in their own healthcare.
They will provide an opportunity for primary care professionals to explore alternative approaches to
blood test communication in a safe and supportive environment.
WP4: Co-designing strategies to improve blood test communication.
1. Co-create an interpretation of study findings with the PPI panel and theatre-makers.
2. Bring together members of the public and primary care staff to collectively develop strategies
to improve blood test communication.
3. Co-produce a workshop resource (for primary care staff) and public engagement materials to
help improve blood test communication and develop patients’ understanding of blood tests.
Methods
Forum Theatre is a participatory performance methodology which allows participants to directly
engage in enacting, critiquing and transforming real-world scenarios. It is one of the tools for social
change described in Augusto Boal’s Theatre of the Oppressed, developed in the 1960s and 1970s
whilst working with marginalised communities in Brazil.53
In a Forum Theatre workshop, actors stage a scene involving conflict or misunderstanding while
audience members (or “spect-actors”) observe and reflect on the social dynamics which emerge.54 A
facilitator then invites spect-actors to suggest alternative actions or responses to change the scene. An
improvised version of the scene then takes place, with spect-actors able to intervene at any time by
asking actors to change their behaviour or stepping into the scene themselves.54
Forum Theatre is increasingly used in healthcare research as a way to explore and change
interpersonal dynamics within healthcare settings.55–57 Like VRE, Forum Theatre provides a space for
participants to see situations from alternate perspectives, inviting them to consider how practices and
ways of working might be transformed.

Co-production process
Part 1
A day-long workshop with the PPI panel and actors from Improbable, an improvisation theatre
company. Using selected research materials (e.g. analytic themes from the meta-ethnography,
ethnographic observations, VRE workshop excerpts), workshop participants will co-design a short
play with multiple scenes, based on the study findings.
Part 2
Two Forum Theatre workshops, each involving approximately 20 local primary care staff and
members of the public, who will be recruited in collaboration with Social Action for Health (a local
community health charity – see Collaborations). The workshops will be led and facilitated by actors
from Improbable, who will perform each scene from the short play in turn.
The play will be professionally recorded, including both the ‘original’ scenes and the adaptations
created in the forum theatre workshops. Scenes in which audience members have participated will not
be used in further outputs except with a participant’s written consent.
Part 3
In two further workshops I will work with the PPI panel to adapt the play into two resources to
disseminate study findings and support improved blood test communication: a workshop for primary
care staff and an online publicly-accessible resource for patients. Both resources will incorporate clips
from the forum theatre exercise, using these as prompts for reflection. I will also work with the PPI
panel to develop and review other public dissemination materials like posters and social media posts.
Analysis
This study will produce a rich and varied dataset including fieldnotes, audio- and video-recorded
consultations, VRE workshop transcripts, and reflexive diaries. In my analysis I will draw upon
linguistic ethnographic approaches, combining insights from ethnographic observation with close
analysis of how language is used in situ by patients and GP staff.58 Linguistic ethnography offers
unique insights into the interpersonal dynamics involved in working practices. Widely used in
education research, it is increasingly recognised as a valuable tool for understanding the role of
language in medical interactions, including in primary care. 37,45,59
NVivo 15 will be used to manage study datasets and CLAN software will be used to support the
transcription of recorded consultations and VRE workshops. To allow me to conduct linguistic
microanalysis of consultation data I will use Jeffersonian notation (following Conversation Analysis
conventions) to capture fine-grained interactional details such as pauses and overlaps.60 I will use an
inductive approach to identify ‘critical moments’ from consultations to be shared in VRE workshops.
6. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
Dissemination of key outputs
This project will produce two main outputs, both adapted (with help from the PPI panel) from the short
play co-produced in WP4:
Workshop resource for clinicians:
 I will deliver the workshop in teaching sessions for local GP practice staff and GP VTS
schemes, and will seek early feedback to refine the workshops and demonstrate impact.
 To ensure the resource reaches clinicians nationally, I will run the workshop at GP
conferences.
 I will share the resource through networks for academic GPs and with the Programme
Directors of GP VTS training schemes across the country.
Online resource for patients:
 This will be hosted on the project webpage and YouTube.

 It will be promoted to the public via social media (e.g. X), flyers at local community groups and
GP practices, and patient participant group mailing lists.
 Key messages from the Forum Theatre workshops will be disseminated to patients in written
form via leaflets and newsletters.
Dissemination of study findings
To patients/the public:
 I will maintain a project webpage on the APOLLO team website throughout the fellowship and
beyond, on which PPI panel members will share summaries of the research plan and findings.
 I will write blogs to be shared on the project website and provide project news via an X
(formerly Twitter) page.
 Guided by the training I will receive in public engagement methods and science
communication, I will produce a podcast for APOLLO team’s podcast, Thinking In Between.
 I will host a stall at QMUL’s Festival of Communities, an annual public engagement event to
showcase research from QMUL and local organisations (with >25,000 attendees since its
inception in 2016).
To all stakeholders:
Together with the PPI panel, and with the support of the QMUL Centre for Public Engagement, I will
host a public dissemination event for local clinicians, community groups and the public. The aim of this
event is to share the research findings and discuss how the resources developed in the project might
be shared to improve blood test communication in primary care.
To academics/clinicians:
 Publication of at least four peer-reviewed open-access papers in target academic journals:
- Meta- ethnography (Sociology of Health and Illness)
- Linguistic ethnography (Social Science and Medicine)
- Video-reflexive ethnography (BJGP)
- Co-producing resources to improve blood testing communication (BMJ Evidence-
Based Medicine)
 Presentations at national conferences (e.g. Society for Academic Primary Care and the Royal
College of General Practitioners).
 Presentations at Preventing Overdiagnosis (an multidisciplinary international conference well-
attended by primary care clinicians) and WONCA (World Organization of Family Doctors).
 Presentations in clinical and academic networks e.g. RCGP Optimal Testing, University of
Bristol Centre for Academic Primary Care, and Wolfson Institute of Population Health.
Anticipated impact
This research will:
i. Prompt lay and professional discussion about the challenges of blood testing and how
they can be overcome;
ii. Reframe the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for the safe and
prudent use of blood testing;
iii. Develop a ‘good practice’ approach to discussing blood tests in primary care, co-
created with lay participants.
Key impacts include:
For patients:
Patients will feel more confident making shared decisions about whether or not to do blood tests, will
have a greater insight into how primary care clinicians approach blood testing, and will understand
their blood test results better.

For primary care staff:
GPs and allied health professionals will feel more confident in supporting patients to make decisions
about doing blood tests and in explaining their results.
For the NHS:
Improving blood test communication and shared decision-making between clinicians and patients is
likely reduce overall numbers of blood tests. This will reduce primary care blood testing costs and
lessen the administrative burden on primary care staff. It will also reduce the costs associated with
repeat and follow-up investigations.
7. Project management
I will be responsible for the project’s progress, budget and outputs, with support from my supervisors
and the QMUL finance team. I have prepared a research timetable (see uploads).
The progress of the project will be monitored through:
Supervision
 Face-to-face meetings with Professor Swinglehurst every fortnight and with Dr Fudge once a
month.
 Monthly online meetings with Dr Watson, with twice-yearly in-person visits in Bristol.
 Whole supervisory team meetings three times a year, with at least one face-to-face meeting in
London per year.
 All supervisors will provide support over email as required and offer feedback on written work.
Project Advisory Group
I have assembled a Project Advisory Group (PAG) which will meet online bi-annually to assess and
guide study progress. PAG members will also act as mentors on an ad-hoc basis. The PAG will be co-
chaired by two lay members, Steven Oliver and name, who helped to develop this proposal. Alongside
my three supervisors, there will be four other PAG members with relevant expertise:
 Professor Natalie Armstrong (Professor of Healthcare Improvement Research, University of
Leicester)
- Medical sociologist with expertise in applied qualitative research and overdiagnosis.
 Dr Julian Treadwell (GP & NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer, University of Bristol)
- Lead for GP Evidence, a co-produced information resource about the benefits and
harms of treatments for long-term conditions.
 Dr Margaret McCartney (GP, writer and Senior Clinical Lecturer in General Practice, University
of St Andrews)
- Internationally-recognised advocate for evidence-based medicine, with extensive
broadcasting experience.
 Dr Jessica Drinkwater (GP & NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer, University of Manchester)
- Academic GP with wide-ranging experience of using participatory and applied theatre
methods to strengthen patient involvement in primary care service improvement.
Formal reviews
In line with QMUL university regulations, I will have regular reviews to assess my progress and identify
any support or training needs:
 Progression review at 6-9 months
 Annual progress reviews at 12, 24, and 36 months
8. Ethics
I will obtain ethical approval from QMUL and the NHS Health Research Authority prior to carrying out
any data collection involving participants. I will take particular ethical precautions with the following
aspects of the project:

Ethnographic observation and recording
I will seek written consent from each member of staff within the GP practice to observe them in their
usual role. I will conduct all observations respectfully and as unobtrusively as possible, leaving a
space immediately if I am asked to do so by staff. In line with usual practice for students observing
patient care, GP practice staff will seek a patient’s explicit consent for me to observe their care, ideally
without me present (e.g. as they collect the patient from the waiting room).61
Video-reflexive ethnography workshops
Staff may feel uncomfortable watching their consultations or be upset by comments made about their
practice by other participants. This risk will be mitigated by ensuring that GP staff are aware of the
purpose of the workshops: i.e., to collectively identify new ways of working, rather than critiquing each
other’s practice. Professor Swinglehurst and Dr Fudge will support me in planning the workshops and
developing a plan for facilitation.
Confidentiality
This project involves the collection of video- and audio-recorded data of clinical consultations as well
as workshops involving GP practice staff. Ensuring the confidentiality and security of data is thus
essential; this was also a high priority for PPI participants. All research data will be securely stored in
line with QMUL’s data management policy, the Data Protection Act 2018, and the conditions of the
Research Ethics Committee approval. Recorded data will be securely stored in encrypted form and
then deleted from the recording device.
9. Success criteria
I will measure the success of my PhD according to the completion of the milestones described in the
attached Gantt chart. The following indicators will be particular measures of success:
1. Ethical approval in place by end of year 1;
2. Meta-ethnographic synthesis submitted for publication by 1st quarter of year 2;
3. Ethnographic data collection and VRE workshops complete by end of year 2;
4. Co-produced resources for patients and clinicians finalised by 3rd quarter of year 3;
5. Application for postdoctoral funding (to scale up and evaluate the resources co-produced in
WP4) in-progress by the end of year 3.
Risks and mitigation
1. Delays in obtaining ethical approval : I will apply for ethical approval promptly upon starting the
fellowship. I will be supported by my supervisors in preparing a high-quality application to
reduce the risk of the initial application being refused.
2. Difficulties completing the meta-ethnography: I have done a rapid scoping search and
ascertained that there is sufficient relevant qualitative literature for a meta-ethnographic
review. I will work with a QMUL librarian to prepare the search strategy and have identified two
relevant training courses. Other members of the APOLLO team will be able to offer
methodological support.
3. Difficulty gaining consent to record consultations : The majority of patients in previous research
gave their consent to the video-recording of GP consultations for research purposes.62
Participants in pre-proposal PPI workshops also felt that recording consultations would be
acceptable to patients. I will use the period of ethnographic observation to build trust with GP
staff prior to making any recordings, and will seek the PPI panel’s guidance on recruitment
materials including consent forms.
4. Challenges in the applied theatre workshops: I anticipate that the forum theatre approach in
WP4 will be new to most of the participants and that some may feel unsure about the aims or
process. This will be mitigated by the expertise of the Improbable actors, who are experienced
in developing improvised theatre productions in collaboration with audiences. I will be
supported by my mentor, Dr Drinkwater (on the Project Advisory Group), who has experience
of using applied theatre methods.



PPI in proposal development (349/350 words)
To decide my research questions and develop this proposal, I held three workshops with 18 members
of the public who had recently had general practice blood tests. Participants were from diverse ethnic
backgrounds, including Black British, South Asian and Chinese, and ranged in age from 20s-70s.
Several had long-term conditions and/or identified as disabled, and two participants were
neurodivergent.
In the first workshop I asked about participants’ experiences of blood testing, which helped shape my
research questions. In later workshops, we discussed the proposed research: the topic, aims and
objectives, methods, and plans for sharing findings. Participants felt strongly that this research was
important. They highlighted communication difficulties throughout the blood testing process:
 “I don’t feel the GP discusses the decision to do a blood test… they just say you need one.”
 “There’s a lot of difficulty in understanding the results without having to go onto Google.”
 “The doctor says ‘we don’t worry when [the blood test] is just out of the range’, but there’s no
time for the GP to properly explain what that means.”
Participants thought that spending several months observing in each general practice would help me
develop trust with staff and patients, making everyone more comfortable with recording consultations.
They liked the idea of using applied theatre methods and felt that a play would be an accessible way of
sharing the research.
Based on feedback from these workshops, I made the following changes to the project:
- I will observe Patient Participation Groups (PPGs) as part of the ethnography and share my
findings in PPG meetings to involve existing patient networks in the research.
- Participants wanted the workshop resource for GPs to be part of GP training, so I will run the
workshop for local GP training programmes.
- I decided not to include GPs in the workshop session to develop the play, as participants felt
that PPI contributors would be able to participate more freely without staff present.
Two workshop participants co-wrote the plain English summary and provided feedback on the PPI
plans. They will co-chair the Project Advisory Group.
PPI in proposed research (348/350 words)
I have assembled a Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) panel who will be involved throughout the
project. The panel is made up of people who have had a blood test in primary care in the past year.
People on the panel are diverse in age, ethnicity and gender and have a range of health experiences.
I will meet with the panel three times a year to discuss study progress and troubleshoot any issues
which come up. I will seek their guidance on recruiting research sites and obtaining consent for
recordings. I will also gather their feedback on early findings from general practice observations. This
will help me choose recordings to show in video-reflexive ethnography workshops.
The PPI panel will work with a theatre company to develop a play based on the research findings,
which will used in theatre workshops with members of the public to find ways to improve blood test
communication. I will recruit participants for the workshops in collaboration with Social Action for
Health, a local charity with extensive experience of working with East London communities who are
not well-represented in research.
I will collaborate with the panel to adapt the play into a workshop (for clinical staff) and a public-facing
online resource (for patients). These resources will support primary care staff and patients to
communicate better about blood tests. The panel will also develop summaries of the research plan
and findings to post on a project webpage. At the end of the project, the PPI panel will help to host a
public event to share our findings with local clinicians and members of the community.
All panel members will be paid for their time and travel expenses in line with NIHR guidance, including
pre-meeting work. I will also offer payments for childcare/caring expenses. The research budget

includes funding for panel members to access research training and to present the research at
conferences.
Two lay participants helped to develop this proposal. They will co-chair a project advisory group with
my supervisors and four external academics/GPs. This group will provide scrutiny, guidance and
oversight throughout the project.

Training and development plan (1006/1000 words)
The QMUL Doctoral College and the Wolfson Institute of Population Health offer a doctoral
development programme with courses covering project management, personal effectiveness, and
research governance. I will attend induction events, yearly cohort days, and research skills seminars
and workshops. As outlined below and in the attached training timetable, I have planned further
training to allow me to successfully complete the PhD and continue my development as a clinical
researcher.
Research skills
Qualitative methodology:
- Advanced Qualitative Research Methods (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175):
Masters module focusing on more complex aspects of qualitative research such as reflexivity,
bias, and advanced data collection methods. This will underpin the qualitative methods training
I received in my MSc and MPhil.
Meta-ethnography (WP1):
To refresh my skills in systematic review and develop my understanding of qualitative synthesis, I will
take the following courses:
- Literature Searching for Systematic Reviews (half-day, QMUL): course on literature searching
in preparation for the meta-ethnography database searches.
- Overview of Qualitative Review and Synthesis Methods (1 day, University of Bristol): course to
aid me in conducting an interpretive qualitative synthesis.
Ethnography and video-reflexive ethnography (WPs2&3):
Building on my previous experience of ethnographic research, I will take the following external
courses:
- Ethnography and Ethnographic Methods (5 days, Essex Summer School in Social Science
Data Analysis, £1,600): module to provide a thorough introduction to the theory and practice of
ethnographic research and set me up for ethnographic observation in GP practices.
- Summer school module on Linguistic Ethnography (5 days, Ghent University, £250): an
introduction to key concepts in linguistic ethnography. This will support me in the close analysis
of recorded data in WP2, building a Conversation Analysis course (University of Oxford) I
completed in 2024.
Creative co-production (WP4):
I will be mentored by Dr Jessica Drinkwater, a member of the PAG who has experience of using forum
theatre to improve primary care services. I will also take the following courses:
- Creative Methods in Qualitative Research (2 days, Social Research Association, £220):
course exploring creative methods for data collection and analysis.
- Forum Theatre Intensive Training (5 days, School of Theatre of the Oppressed, £850):
intensive course to develop skills in applied theatre methods.
Dissemination and impact
Patient and public involvement/engagement:
To support me in working with public contributors throughout the project I will take the following
courses:
- Co-design, Co-production and Participatory Action Research; Inclusivity within Research
Processes (1 day each, University of Bristol)
- Hands on Engagement: Taking Research to Events and Festivals (1 day, QMUL): course to
prepare me to host the dissemination event.
Implementation of findings:

- Knowledge into Action (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175), led by Professor Carl
Heneghan. This module will support my plans for impact and dissemination, helping me to
understand how my recommendations can be implemented in clinical practice. It will also
inform an application for postdoctoral research to develop and test interventions to improve
blood test communication.
Disseminating findings:
- Science Communication Masterclass (4 days, UWE Bristol, £400) : intensive course on
communicating research findings with the public. This will help me in sharing my PhD research
via podcasts, blogs and social media.
- WordPress website design (1 day, CityLit, £129) – course to help me design a project website
to share study findings and patient resources.
Conferences:
I will attend and present work at conferences including:
- Royal College of General Practitioners
- Society of Academic Primary Care
- World Organization of Family Doctors (WONCA)
- Preventing Overdiagnosis: an international conference with a strong primary care presence
Development as a researcher
Leadership:
- Emerging Clinical Leaders (6 days in total, King’s Fund, £2712): interprofessional programme
to build skills in leadership, resilience and team management. This will further my development
as an independent research leader.
Teaching:
- To develop as an educator I will facilitate undergraduate/Masters module seminars within the
department when my research commitments allow. I will attend Graduate Teaching Assistant
Development workshops provided by QMUL.
Research networks:
I will be a member of the following research networks, allowing me to learn from other researchers, get
feedback on my work, and develop new collaborations:
At QMUL:
- APOLLO Social Sciences team:
 Multidisciplinary team of qualitative primary care researchers, led by Professor
Swinglehurst. Alongside regular team meetings I will have the opportunity to attend
APOLLO’s termly writing retreat.
- Thinking Between the Lines:
 Qualitative research network, co-convened by Dr Fudge, holding monthly meetings for
researchers to present work-in-progress and receive feedback.
Externally:
- Preventing Overdiagnosis Early/Mid-Career Researcher Network:
 International network of researchers working on overdiagnosis, which provided helpful
feedback on this proposal.
- RCGP Optimal Testing group:
 Subgroup of the RCGP Overdiagnosis interest group, led by Dr Watson. I will attend
their tri-yearly meetings to share my work and discuss how to improve primary care
blood testing practices.

Research visit
In the 3rd year of the PhD, I will spend six weeks in Australia, visiting two institutions which are
pioneering work on communicating overdiagnosis to patients and developing patient education
resources. In each site I will be mentored by the unit lead and a mid-career researcher. We will agree a
timetable in advance of my visit.
 Sydney Health Literacy Lab , University of Sydney
o The Lab focuses on healthcare communication to prevent overdiagnosis and low-value
care. I will spend four weeks here, hosted by Professor Carissa Bonner, a behavioural
scientist who specialises in health literacy and risk communication, and Dr Brooke
Nickel.
o I will use insights from the Lab’s extensive work co-producing health literacy tools to aid
the development of the WP4 resource for patients.
 Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values (ACHEEV), University of
Wollongong.
o I will spend two weeks at ACHEEV, hosted by Professor Stacy Carter and Dr Patti Shih.
The ACHEEV team aims to find solutions to medical overuse by making evidence more
accessible.
o I am a technical advisor for Dr Shih’s project developing an online resource to explain
blood test results to Australian patients, and will be able to develop this collaboration
during my visit.

Supervisor (250 words per supervisor)
Professor Deborah Swinglehurst (250/250)
Deborah Swinglehurst is a Professor of Primary Care and general practitioner. Her academic work
uses qualitative methods, including ethnography and linguistic ethnography, to explore how
interaction and social relationships shape healthcare practices. She leads the APOLLO social science
group, adopting innovative methodologies at the interfaces of medicine, social science and linguistics
to address complex healthcare challenges.
She is Institute co-lead for Applied Social Science within the QMUL Primary Care Unit and sits on the
Executive Board and Research Committee. She is in the senior management team for the Wellcome-
funded Doctoral Training Programme on Health Data in Practice and the leadership team for QMUL’s
NIHR SPCR membership.
Professor Swinglehurst has published widely in journals including BMJ, BMJ Quality & Safety, BJGP,
and Social Science and Medicine. She is on the 2024 Stanford/Elsevier Top 2% Scientists list of the
most cited scientists globally, and was awarded the 2019 RCGP/SAPC John Fry Award for her
contribution to general practice. Her recent NIHR-funded research on multimorbidity and
polypharmacy involved co-producing a suite of RCGP e-learning modules (reaching >1000 learners)
and a public engagement resource (Let’s Talk Differently about Medicines) which has reached >1800
people.
She will provide expertise in meta-ethnography, ethnographic approaches (including linguistic
ethnography/video-reflexive ethnography), and creative co-production. We will have supervision
meetings fortnightly and she will provide further support as needed.
Accredited by the UK Council for Graduate Education as a recognised research supervisor, she has
supervised 6 PhD students to completion, currently supervises 6 PhD students, and has examined 10
PhDs.
Dr Jessica Watson (250/250)
Jessica Watson is a general practitioner and NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer whose research uses
mixed methods to understand and improve primary care blood testing practices. She chairs the
Primary Care Academic Collaborative and leads the Optimal Testing division of the RCGP
Overdiagnosis group.
Dr Watson has been awarded three consecutive NIHR Fellowships and has delivered over £2m of
research funding. She has authored 64 peer-reviewed research articles which have been cited over
2,100 times. Her work has been featured on BBC radio, ITV news, and in BMJ, BJGP and RCGP
podcasts. She was Principal Investigator on the NIHR-funded Test Talk Tools study (2022-2024), and
currently leads two further NIHR-funded studies:
- Accessible Results (Principal Investigator): qualitative project developing tools to help patients
with diverse needs access and understand online blood test results.
- Optimal testing for long-term condition monitoring in primary care (Joint Lead Applicant): five-
year mixed-methods study to develop, implement and evaluate a new blood test monitoring
strategy for long-term conditions.
Dr Watson will provide expertise in blood testing and overdiagnosis as well as mentorship in research
dissemination. She will offer opportunities to collaborate with researchers at the University of Bristol
and within PACT. We will meet online monthly and I will travel to Bristol for in-person supervision
twice-yearly. We will have joint online meetings with Professor Swinglehurst and Dr Fudge three times
a year to ensure regular contact between all supervisors.
She is currently supervising 1 PhD student. She has supervised 2 GP ACFs and 3 MSc students.
Dr Nina Fudge (250/250)
Dr Fudge is a Lecturer in Social Science and THIS Institute Research Fellow with a background in
social anthropology. Her research explores knowledge, expertise and practice in complex healthcare
settings such as community pharmacies and GP practices. Her recent research focuses on patients’

and clinicians’ experiences and practices of polypharmacy, aiming to develop safe and person-
centred approaches to primary care medicines use.
She has extensive experience of qualitative participant-focused methods, including narrative,
ethnographic and visual approaches. She has particular expertise in video-reflexive ethnography
(VRE) which she has used in multiple polypharmacy research projects, including her THIS Fellowship
(awarded 2021) which uses VRE to improve the delivery of structured medication reviews.
She has worked closely with Professor Swinglehurst since 2017 as part of the APOLLO-MM project,
including on the co-production of patient-facing resources to encourage discussions around
polypharmacy. She is Deputy Director of Graduate Studies for the Wolfson Institute of Population
Health and co-convenes Thinking Between the Lines, a qualitative research network through which I
will receive feedback on my work and take part in data analysis workshops.
I will meet with Dr Fudge on a monthly basis throughout the fellowship. She will provide particular
guidance on hosting the VRE workshops in WP3 and analysing the resulting data. With her experience
of co-design methods she will also support me in co-producing the clinician and patient resources in
WP4.
She is currently supervising 3 PhD students (1 with Professor Swinglehurst) and has supervised 3
PhD students to completion (2 with Professor Swinglehurst).
Collaborations (627/600 words)
Academic collaborations
Centre for Academic Primary Care (CAPC), University of Bristol
Through Dr Watson I will develop links with CAPC and their ongoing blood testing research, including
the Accessible Results study – a participatory study which explores how people with diverse needs
can be supported to access blood test results online. Through working with CAPC I hope to foster
cross-institutional collaboration on a project Professor Swinglehurst is developing which explores GP
blood testing organisational routines.
Primary care Academic Collaborative (PACT)
PACT is a UK network of primary care researchers which has run several studies relevant to the
proposed PhD: the Why Test study, investigating the indication for primary care blood tests; and the
Hidden Workload study, exploring primary care clinician workload. I hope to collaborate with PACT to
disseminate the research findings, including the workshop for GP staff, and will explore the possibility
of developing a spin-off project exploring blood testing communication nationally.
Sydney Health Literacy Lab/ Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values
I will collaborate with these two centres which work in partnership as part of Wiser Healthcare
(https://www.wiserhealthcare.org.au/), a research initiative to reduce overdiagnosis in Australia and
worldwide. The research visit will allow for a mutual exchange of methodological approaches. The
Sydney Health Literacy Lab hosts a number of behavioural scientists from whom I hope to learn about
implementation science and resource development. At ACHEEV, I will learn about their empirical
ethics work using community juries to define and make judgements about overdiagnosis. In return, I
will be able to share my experience of using ethnographic methods to understand how overdiagnosis
emerges in practice.
Clinical collaborations
Dr Stefán Hjörleifsson, Choosing Wisely Norway
Led by GP and academic Dr Hjörleifsson, Choosing Wisely Norway is an initiative to encourage
clinicians and patients to discuss when investigations and treatments are useful and when they are
not. In Norway there is strong professional commitment to avoiding unnecessary testing, and the
Norwegian College of General Practice adopted a position paper on overdiagnosis in 2016 – the first

of its kind to be adopted by a national medical college. I am working with Dr Hjörleifsson on a workshop
for WONCA 2025 and will continue my collaboration with him throughout the fellowship. In the 2nd year
of the PhD I will visit Dr Hjörleifsson and the GP academic community at the University of Bergen to
learn more about their work advocating for prudent testing in primary care in Norway and consider how
this could be translated to a UK context.
Lab Tests Online (UK), NHS Digital, and Pathology Tests Explained (Australia)
My supervisor Dr Watson works closely with Lab Tests Online, and my ACHEEV mentor Dr Shih is
undertaking a project with Pathology Tests Explained (the Australian counterpart), on which I am a
technical advisor. These organisations provide online resources to help patients understand their
blood test results. Dr Watson has also been collaborating with NHS Digital on the Accessible Results
study to provide guidance on NHS app design for online results access. I will work with these three
organisations to feedback my research findings and provide recommendations of how online blood
test resources could be improved.
Community collaborations
Social Action for Health (SAfH): East London community health charity.
SAfH have extensive experience running community health research projects and focus groups in
collaboration with NHS and academic partners. I will work with SAfH to assemble a group of people to
participate in the Forum Theatre workshops in WP4.
Improbable: Improvisation-based theatre company (https://www.improbable.co.uk/)
Alongside producing award-winning West End shows, Improbable has a research wing which uses
improvisation to interpret research findings and bring them to new audiences. Improbable’s Executive
Director has agreed to collaborate on the project. Theatre-makers from Improbable will help to design
the play and facilitate the Forum Theatre workshops.
